content.text: Generics parameterize types so one class or function safely handles many payload types. Protocols define structural interfaces without inheritance — enabling duck-typed backends that pass mypy. Together they are the two most powerful typing tools for writing modular, extensible agent systems.
let a single class or function work with different types while preserving type relationships.
binds it to a class. The type checker tracks the concrete type through the substitution, so

) describe required methods and attributes — structural subtyping. Any class that implements those members satisfies the Protocol without explicitly inheriting from it. This models Python's natural duck typing while enabling mypy to verify it statically.
Both features are purely static: they carry no runtime overhead and require no changes to concrete classes that already have the right shape.

Agent systems compose heterogeneous components: different LLM providers, multiple retrieval backends, pluggable memory stores, and optional tracers. Without structural interfaces, every component must inherit from a shared base class, creating tight coupling and making it hard to integrate third-party libraries.
Generics solve the dual problem of result containers. A naive approach types tool outputs as
, which silently propagates errors. A generic
keeps the payload type tracked from the tool all the way to the orchestrator, so a downstream function that expects
will fail at type-check time — not at runtime.
— Protocols let you type "anything with search(query, limit)" without a shared base class
— swap LLM provider, vector store, or tracer by matching the Protocol signature, no inheritance tree changes
— a stub class satisfying a Protocol requires no
— Protocol checks are opt-in; existing code with no annotations still works

and only verify method/attribute existence, not signatures
) and contravariant TypeVars are rarely needed in agent code but can cause confusing errors
— Python erases generic parameters at runtime;
— for a single-file agent script that will not grow, annotating with
or inline types is often more practical than introducing Protocols

is a placeholder for a concrete type. When a generic function is called, the type checker substitutes the TypeVar with the inferred or explicit type. The key rule: every use of the same TypeVar within one function signature must resolve to the same type.

# ── TypeVar & generic functions ──────────────────────────────────────────────
# Python 3.12+ supports the compact "type T" syntax, but TypeVar works on 3.9+.
from typing import TypeVar, Generic
from collections.abc import Callable, Sequence

T = TypeVar("T")
U = TypeVar("U")

def first(items: list[T]) -> T | None:
    """Return the first element or None — mypy knows return type matches list element type."""
    return items[0] if items else None

def transform(items: list[T], fn: Callable[[T], U]) -> list[U]:
    """Map items through fn preserving input/output type relationships."""
    return [fn(item) for item in items]

# --- usage ---
ids: list[str] = ["doc-1", "doc-2", "doc-3"]
first_id: str | None = first(ids)          # mypy: str | None  ✓
lengths: list[int] = transform(ids, len)   # mypy: list[int]   ✓

scores: list[float] = [0.9, 0.7, 0.5]
top: float | None = first(scores)          # mypy: float | None ✓

argument restricts a TypeVar to a specific type or Protocol. The function body can then call any method promised by the bound. This is the bridge between Generics and Protocols:
means "any T that satisfies HasEmbedding", and the return type is still

Protocol-typed functions accept stub objects with the right shape — no mock framework needed. Generic result types make assertions straightforward because the type is tracked explicitly.

The diagram below shows how Protocols and Generics cooperate in a typical agent system. Protocols (left) define the interfaces for each component type; Generic containers (centre) wrap typed payloads; the Agent (right) combines both without knowing the concrete implementations.

Generics and Protocols are erased at runtime. A call to
executes exactly like a call to an untyped dataclass — there is no dispatch overhead, no boxing, and no reflection. The type information exists only during static analysis.

checks: these iterate over the Protocol's member names. For a Protocol with three methods, the check takes microseconds — negligible in typical agent loops, but avoidable in tight inner loops by doing the check once at registration time.
have no additional memory overhead compared to plain dataclasses of the same fields. Scaling to millions of tool calls per day introduces zero typing-related cost.

Type annotations do not substitute for input validation, but they make it easier to see where validation is missing. A
makes it obvious that the payload must be a list — a guard at the LLM response parser can be narrow and precise.
Protocol-typed interfaces help isolate untrusted backend implementations. A sandbox executor that satisfies
boundary — the agent never holds a direct reference to the executor's internals. This is not a security boundary in itself, but the structural interface makes it easier to audit and mock.

are capability checklists. A class passes the checklist if it has the required methods with the right signatures — it does not need a family name. If it quacks, swims, and flies like an LLM client, the type checker calls it a
TypeVar and Generic[T] parameterize containers and result wrappers, eliminating Any in typed agent pipelines
Protocol defines structural interfaces for pluggable backends — LLM clients, retrievers, memory stores, tracers
Bound TypeVars (bound=SomeProtocol) constrain what the type parameter can be while preserving the concrete return type
@runtime_checkable enables isinstance() validation for Plugin registries; it checks shape, not signature
Protocols have zero runtime overhead — they live only in the type checker's world
Prefer Protocol over ABC when integrating third-party code that you cannot modify to add inheritance
Use collections.abc (Iterable, Callable, Mapping) as ready-made structural interfaces before writing your own
Run mypy or pyright in CI to catch Protocol violations early — generics and protocols are only as useful as the static analysis that enforces them
